title: Proc SQL & Data Step Best Practices

rules.title: Pull with inline subquery, then join for additional fields
rules.body: Pull claims using an inline subquery in the WHERE clause first, then join for additional fields afterward. This avoids inefficient claim pulls. Also use the &med_varis. macro to select only needed variables; there should be no need to pull entire claim lines.

rules.title: Avoid PROC SORT combined with NODUPKEY as a general rule
rules.body: NODUPKEY makes it hard to know what rows were dropped. When SAS encounters multiple rows with the exact same values for those BY variables, it retains the first occurrence it finds and deletes the rest. So you want to make sure you are aware of your deduplication grain.

rules.title: Use FIRST. DATA step logic for top-ranked row
rules.body: Typically you will sort by the ID of interest and the ranking variable, then use FIRST.ID in a DATA step to retain exactly one record per ID.
list: Step 1 — Sort by ID, then ranking variable (ascending or descending as needed)
Step 2 — Use a DATA step with FIRST.studyid logic to retain only the first record per studyid

rules.title: Use SELECT DISTINCT in most PROC SQL queries
rules.body: Unless there is a deliberate documented reason not to. This surfaces unintended fan-out early — if row count > distinct ID count, something is causing duplication and we can investigate before continuing.
rules.tip: Add the commented-out row count from the log next to the code that created the table. This lets validators confirm they are on the same track. Not needed for every table, but definitely at key tables.
